resume:
MID-LEVEL DATA ENGINEER — Analytics & Streaming Platforms

PROFILE
Data engineer with six years of experience building and operating batch and streaming data platforms for consumer-facing products. I have spent most of my career owning end-to-end pipelines: ingestion, transformation, warehouse modeling, orchestration, and the data-quality layer that keeps downstream analysts and machine-learning teams unblocked. I am comfortable across the modern data stack and equally comfortable being paged at three in the morning when a pipeline silently drops half its events. My core belief is that a data platform is only as valuable as it is trusted, and trust comes from observability, idempotency, and honest SLAs rather than from heroics.

PROFESSIONAL EXPERIENCE

Orbital Commerce — Data Engineer II, Platform Data team (2022 to present)
I work on the team that owns the central event pipeline feeding the analytics warehouse and the real-time personalization service for an e-commerce platform processing tens of millions of events per day. I report to the Data Engineering Manager and partner closely with analytics engineers, the personalization ML team, and product analysts.
- Re-architected the clickstream ingestion pipeline from a fragile cron-driven batch design to a streaming design built on Kafka and a stream-processing layer, because the batch pipeline could not meet the freshness requirement of the personalization team. I designed the topic partitioning scheme, the schema-registry contracts, and the dead-letter handling for malformed events. I owned the design document and drove the review across three teams.
- Built the data-quality framework that runs assertion checks on every critical table after each load. I defined the check types (row-count anomalies, null-rate drift, referential integrity, freshness), wired them into the orchestration DAGs, and routed failures to an on-call rotation with severity tiers. Before this framework, bad data routinely reached dashboards before anyone noticed.
- Owned the warehouse modeling for the order and fulfillment domains. I designed the dimensional model, wrote the transformation layer in dbt, and worked with analysts to deprecate a tangle of redundant legacy tables. I led the migration that moved roughly forty downstream queries onto the new models.
- Improved the cost profile of our warehouse spend by partitioning and clustering the largest fact tables and rewriting several of the most expensive recurring queries. I built the query-cost attribution dashboard that made it visible which teams and which queries drove spend.
- Acted as the primary on-call engineer for the platform on a rotating basis, handling incidents ranging from upstream schema changes that broke ingestion to a region-wide warehouse degradation. I wrote the postmortems for the incidents I owned.

Lamplight Media — Data Engineer (2019 to 2022)
I joined a small data team supporting a subscription media product and helped grow the platform from a handful of hand-maintained scripts into a real orchestrated system. I reported to the Head of Data.
- Built the company's first orchestrated ETL using a workflow scheduler, replacing a collection of cron jobs that no one fully understood. I migrated the existing jobs one domain at a time to avoid a big-bang cutover.
- Designed and implemented the subscription-events pipeline that fed the finance team's revenue reporting. Because the numbers fed financial reporting, I built reconciliation checks that compared pipeline output against the billing system of record.
- Built the experimentation data mart that the product team used to read out A/B tests, defining the metrics layer and the assignment-deduplication logic.
- Mentored a junior analyst transitioning into engineering, pairing with them on their first production pipeline.

Brightwater Analytics (Contract) — Junior Data Engineer (2018 to 2019)
My first data role, a contract position where I supported a consultancy's client deliverables.
- Built ingestion connectors for a variety of third-party marketing APIs and normalized the data into a shared schema.
- Handled the messy realities of third-party data: rate limits, inconsistent pagination, and partner APIs that silently changed their response shapes without warning. I learned early that the source you do not control is the source that will eventually break you, which is why I now treat schema validation at ingestion as non-negotiable.
- Wrote the documentation and runbooks that the client's internal team used after handoff, because a pipeline that only its author can operate is a liability rather than an asset.

TECHNICAL SKILLS
Languages: Python, SQL, some Scala. Streaming and messaging: Kafka, stream-processing frameworks. Orchestration: workflow schedulers and DAG-based tooling. Warehouse and transformation: cloud data warehouses, dbt, dimensional modeling. Infrastructure: containerization, infrastructure-as-code basics, CI/CD for data. Observability: data-quality assertion frameworks, lineage, alerting. I care a great deal about idempotency, backfill safety, and schema evolution because these are where data pipelines quietly rot.

HOW I WORK
I start from the consumer of the data and work backward to the source. I write design docs before I write pipelines. I prefer boring, observable systems over clever ones. I treat data contracts between teams as real contracts and I push back when an upstream team wants to change a schema without notice. I believe the data-quality layer is not optional infrastructure but the thing that earns a platform the right to be trusted.

EDUCATION
Bachelor's degree in Computer Science. Ongoing self-directed study in distributed systems and stream processing.
jd: We are hiring a mid-level Data Engineer to join the team that owns our central event pipeline and analytics warehouse for a high-traffic consumer product processing tens of millions of events daily. Reporting to the Data Engineering Manager, you will own pipelines end to end: streaming and batch ingestion, warehouse modeling, orchestration, and the data-quality layer. Responsibilities include designing and operating Kafka-based streaming ingestion, building dimensional models and transformation layers, owning data-quality and observability tooling, managing warehouse cost and performance, and participating in an on-call rotation. We want an engineer who can clearly describe a system they personally designed, explain the specific tradeoffs behind their design decisions, and quantify the outcome of their work — whether that is freshness, cost, reliability, or data-quality improvement. Requirements: four or more years building production data pipelines, strong SQL and Python, hands-on experience with streaming systems and a modern warehouse, and a track record of owning data quality.

history.q: Walk me through your current role at Orbital: team, who you report to, the scale of the pipeline, and what you personally own.
You re-architected clickstream ingestion from batch to streaming. What specifically forced that change — what was the batch pipeline failing to do?
Walk me through your partitioning scheme for the Kafka topics. Why did you partition the way you did?
How did you handle malformed events? Describe the dead-letter mechanism you built.
On the data-quality framework: walk me through one specific check type and exactly how it decides something is anomalous.
Give me a real incident where that framework caught bad data before it hit a dashboard. What happened?
You migrated roughly forty queries onto new dbt models. How did you make sure you didn't silently change a number an analyst depended on?
On cost: which query or table drove the most spend, and what specifically did you change?
Tell me about the hardest on-call incident you personally owned — root cause and your fix.
At Lamplight you built reconciliation checks against the billing system. What did you do when the pipeline and billing disagreed?
Across all this work, where have you made a design decision you later regretted, and why?
Let's close the loop on the batch-to-streaming rewrite. You've told me the design. Now: after it shipped, what did freshness actually go to, and what did that unlock?

candidate_last_answer: Right, so the batch-to-streaming rewrite. The situation was that our clickstream pipeline ran on an hourly cron, so the personalization team was making decisions on data that could be up to an hour stale, and for things like 'recommend based on what you just viewed' that's basically useless. My task was to get fresh events into both the warehouse and the real-time service. So what I actually did: I stood up Kafka with a partition key on user-id so that all events for a session land in order on the same partition, I defined Avro schemas in a schema registry so producers couldn't ship breaking changes, and I built a dead-letter topic so malformed events got quarantined instead of poisoning the consumer. I also wrote the consumer to be idempotent using an event-id dedup key so replays during a backfill wouldn't double-count. The design went through review across three teams and I owned the doc. It was a pretty clean cutover — we ran the old batch path in parallel for a couple of weeks to compare before we cut over.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: It was a pretty clean cutover

expected-yield: the concrete post-launch freshness figure (e.g. from ~1 hour down to seconds/minutes) and the measurable downstream effect it unlocked for personalization, supplying the Result the candidate omitted from an otherwise complete S/T/A account
